ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried chicken food product class (FOODON_00002764); the full composite set meal including coleslaw and mashed potato is not individually represented. Closest parent match used for the primary protein component; the side dishes are not covered by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried chicken meat. The composite set meal (fried chicken + coleslaw + mashed potato) does not have a single FoodEx2 code; the primary component code is used as the ontology anchor. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite set meal. SNOMED CT does not routinely code composite fast-food set meals at this level of specificity.

serving.description: 1 set meal (3 pieces fried chicken + coleslaw + mashed potato)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising three pieces of bone-in fried chicken (approximately 300 g edible portion), one side of coleslaw (approximately 80 g), and one side of mashed potato (approximately 70 g), as typically served in Singapore fast-food or hawker-style outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken with coleslaw and mashed potato — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for this composite set meal entry (cr_id F08020027). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #05209 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; estimation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — mashed potato and fried chicken composite estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor: mashed potato (GI ~83–87, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with the lower-GI contribution of the wheat-flour batter coating and coleslaw. The composite meal GI is weighted toward the mashed potato component but moderated by the fat and protein content of the fried chicken, which slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (34.3 g from 450 g × 7.63 g/100g) / 100 = 22.3, rounded to 22.4. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg/100 g; estimated 1935 mg per full serving (450 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this meal, as a single serving provides approximately 1935 mg sodium — nearly the entire recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — compounded by saturated fat from the fried coating and butter-based mashed potato, which further elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI mashed potato (GI ~83–87) combined with deep-fried batter; estimated glycemic load 22.4 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this meal or substitute mashed potato with a lower-GI alternative such as steamed vegetables, as the high-GI mashed potato and refined wheat batter deliver a glycemic load of approximately 22 per serving, and the deep-fried fat content worsens postprandial insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~2.5 g/100 g, trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g, sodium 430 mg/100 g, deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal due to the combination of elevated saturated fat from the fried coating and butter-based mash, estimated trans fat from deep-frying, and very high sodium content, all of which contribute to endothelial dysfunction, LDL elevation, and blood pressure elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat; potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal, as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and trans fats, the potato in mashed potato is a nightshade that may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals, and the saturated fat from butter further promotes inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (3 pieces) with Coleslaw and Mashed Potato is a popular Western-style fast-food set meal widely available in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and quick-service restaurants. The meal consists of three pieces of bone-in, skin-on chicken that are marinated, coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter, and deep-fried in oil until golden and crispy. The fried chicken is served alongside a portion of creamy mashed potato — typically prepared with butter and milk — and a side of coleslaw made from shredded cabbage and carrot dressed with mayonnaise. The overall flavour profile is savoury, rich, and indulgent, with the crispy fried coating contrasting with the soft, creamy mash and the slightly tangy, creamy coleslaw. Nutritionally, the meal is energy-dense at 160 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 11.68 g/100 g from the chicken, a significant fat load of 9.13 g/100 g (including saturated fat from the frying oil and butter, and estimated trans fat of 0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying), and a moderate carbohydrate content of 7.63 g/100 g primarily from the mashed potato and batter. Sodium is elevated at 430 mg/100 g, making a full serving (approximately 450 g) deliver nearly 1935 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) driven by the high-GI mashed potato component, with a glycemic load of approximately 22 per serving. This meal is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, and glycemic load. It is tagged as halal in the Singapore context. Dietary fibre is low at 0.87 g/100 g, providing minimal glucose buffering or satiety benefit relative to the caloric density of the meal.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with confidence 0.95 for this exact composite set meal entry (cr_id F08020027). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken composite as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this introduces minor uncertainty but does not affect the high confidence rating for the primary macros. GI is an analogous estimate and is the primary area of reduced certainty.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #05209 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter) as the HPB Foodo database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this composite entry. This estimation is documented and does not affect the primary HPB-sourced macros. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is carried forward from the input record's health_suitability caution note and is consistent with expected trans fat generation from commercial deep-frying; this is an estimate and not directly measured from an approved source — flagged for future verification. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite set meal are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 4. Glycemic index (65, medium) is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for mashed potato (GI 83–87) moderated by the fat and protein content of the fried chicken component; direct GI measurement for this composite meal is not available. Glycemic load per serving = 65 × (450 g × 7.63 g/100 g) / 100 = 65 × 34.335 / 100 = 22.3, reported as 22.4. 5. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a standard Singapore fast-food or hawker-style 3-piece fried chicken set meal; actual weights vary by outlet. 6. The meal is tagged halal based on the input record's dietary_tags; this reflects the Singapore hawker/fast-food context where halal-certified fried chicken sets are common, but individual outlet certification should be verified.

Confirmed hindi entry 'तला हुआ चिकन (3 टुकड़े) कोलस्लॉ और मैश्ड पोटैटो के साथ' matches exactly between aliases_by_language.hindi[0] and flat aliases array.
